---
title: "Chapter 7: Drill planning under uncertainty"
subtitle: "POMDP planning with Monte Carlo Tree Search on the BC Golden Triangle regional prospectivity prior"
sidebar: drill_planning
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## What this chapter is about

The earlier chapters build prospectivity maps. A prospectivity
map is a per-cell probability surface, and it doesn't tell you
where to drill *next* given what you've already drilled. Real
exploration campaigns are sequential: each hole costs money,
returns information, and that information should update the
next-hole decision.

This chapter ports the methodology of *Intelligent Prospector*
(Mern et al., *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289, 2023) onto the BC
Golden Triangle regional prospectivity prior from Chapter 2: a
POMDP planner with Monte Carlo Tree Search (POMCP), asked to
sequentially drill within a fixed budget against three baseline
policies. The Bear Cub property-scale GP drill-site recommender
that uses the same general idea at single-claim scale lives in
[Chapter 1](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html).

![POMCP discovery curves on the BC Golden Triangle prior. Each line is a different policy; the y-axis is the fraction of true deposits drilled after k holes. POMCP recovers more of the prior's mass per hole than uncertainty-sampling or random baselines.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_pomcp_discovery_curves.png)

## BCGT POMDP decision planning

The methodological reference is *Intelligent Prospector* v1.0 (Mern
et al., *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289, 2023). The setup:

- **State** = per-cell deposit indicator across a 30×30 working
  subarea (~15 km × 15 km centered on the BCGT prior's centroid
  of known occurrences). Cells are i.i.d. Bernoulli draws from
  the prior.
- **Observation** = noiseless reveal of the cell's true state
  when drilled. (v1.0 simplification; v2.0 adds measurement
  noise.)
- **Action** = drill cell (x, y), or stop and bank reward.
- **Reward** = $V per true positive drilled minus $C per drill.
- **Policy** = POMCP (Monte Carlo Tree Search over the belief
  MDP).

Four policies side-by-side on the same prior: random (uniform
over unrevealed cells), greedy (drill the highest-prior cell),
EOI (drill the cell that reduces uncertainty about the deposit
indicator the most, per Caers et al. 2022,
DOI [10.1007/s11053-022-10030-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11053-022-10030-1)),
and POMCP. The result on this prior is that greedy and EOI tie
and both outperform POMCP, which beats random only slightly. The
greedy / EOI overlap is mechanical. Every BCGT cell sits below
50% probability, where binary entropy is monotone increasing in p,
so the hit-maximizing objective (greedy) and the
information-maximizing objective (EOI) pick the same cell
cell-by-cell.

The greedy-beats-POMCP ordering looks counterintuitive but is
structurally expected for this setup. With i.i.d. Bernoulli cells
and a noiseless sensor, the optimal sequential policy IS one-step
greedy: each drill collapses one cell's belief to truth and tells
the model nothing about any other cell, so there is no multi-step
lookahead value to extract. POMCP's MCTS rollouts spend planning
budget exploring non-greedy actions (via UCB bonus) that have no
expected payoff in this regime, and those exploration picks are
what you see as the gap below the greedy curve. POMCP's planning
machinery is the right tool for a different problem than the
v1.0 setup gives it.

The v2.0 extensions in Mern et al. 2024 ([arXiv 2410.10610](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10610))
change that. Correlated draws (a Gaussian random field over the
prior surface, thresholded) mean an observation does update
neighbor priors, so multi-step lookahead becomes meaningful and
POMCP's MCTS starts paying its way. Noisy sensors mean a drill
observation returns a likelihood rather than collapsing a cell to
truth, so planning over belief states becomes necessary. Once
correlated updates push some neighbor priors above 0.5 the
greedy / EOI overlap also breaks: greedy keeps drilling the
high-probability cluster while EOI moves to the still-uncertain
margins. The notebook ships a small v2.0 toy on a synthetic
3-hypothesis prior demonstrating the belief-update mechanics
including the all-hypotheses-wrong falsification case.

## What this piece does and doesn't establish

The POMDP work is a methodology demonstration on a regional prior,
not a production-grade resource model. The path-planning policy is
single-hypothesis: the prior is fixed and is not updated mid-campaign,
which is the v1.0 simplification rather than the v2.0 multi-hypothesis
extension. Real exploration would also need to handle drill-cost
gradients (terrain, access), real per-hole information yield
distributions rather than the simulated ones I use here, and the
practical constraint that drill rigs cost money to move. The notebook
documents these limits explicitly.

The property-scale counterpart in [Chapter 1](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)
produces a workable next-hole pair on the 24 Bear Cub holes even
though the spatial-only Gaussian process predicts pay-zone grade
poorly in absolute terms (negative leave-one-out R², because the
beach-line geology creates a gradient that 24 samples cannot
constrain). The recommender output is still meaningful because the
GP posterior variance is a faithful map of where the data is sparse,
even when the GP mean is unreliable.

## Where this goes from here

This is an early experiment at converting a regional probability
surface into a hole-location decision. The BCGT POMDP runs on a
prior covering thousands of square kilometers. The underlying
notebook documents the v1.0 simplifications I made; the v2.0
extensions in Mern et al. 2024
([arXiv 2410.10610](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10610)) cover
correlated draws, noisy sensors, and multi-hypothesis
falsification. Those would widen the POMCP-vs-greedy gap and let
the planner detect when its geological hypotheses are wrong. The
notebook ships a small toy implementation of the v2.0 belief-
update mechanics on a synthetic 3-hypothesis prior, showing the
posterior trajectory under each ground-truth scenario including
the "all hypotheses wrong" falsification case.

A production-scale Python port of the v2.0 paper is the next
release on this repo. The milestones I have queued: B.1
correlated-prior single-hypothesis POMCP on the BCGT prior; B.2
retrospective validation against the BCGS post-2010 drill record
(my contribution beyond Mern, since the paper validates on
synthetic Monte Carlo only); C.1 Bernoulli noisy sensor with a
3-by-3 sensitivity sweep on (alpha, beta); C.2 multi-hypothesis
posterior over four paper hypotheses plus a null, with the
falsification check that fires when h_0 dominates the posterior.
Solver split: POMCP via `pomdp_py` for B.1, B.2, and C.1
(matches this chapter's v1.0 setup); SARSOP via the APPL
`pomdpsol` binary for C.2 (matches the paper directly; the
build script lives at `scripts/build_pomdpsol.sh`).

The property-scale counterpart of this idea, a GP drill-site
recommender on the 24 Bear Cub holes, sits in
[Chapter 1](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html).

## Deeper notebooks

- [BCGT POMDP decision planning](./notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html): full notebook with POMCP implementation, random and greedy baselines, and the v1.0-vs-v2.0 limits discussion.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
